items.myth: Non-convex means gradient descent always fails.
items.reality: Non-convex only removes global guarantees; SGD with noise, momentum, and good hyperparameters often finds useful minima in practice, especially in overparameterized models.
items.example: ResNet training on ImageNet is non-convex, yet SGD reliably reaches low training loss across seeds when learning rate and initialization are tuned.

items.myth: If the training loss is convex, the model is convex.
items.reality: Convexity is about the loss as a function of parameters for fixed data; a neural network can use convex component losses (MSE) while the composed map w -> loss remains non-convex in w.
items.example: MSE is convex in predictions, but MSE(relu(X w), y) is non-convex in w because ReLU introduces piecewise linear kinks.

items.myth: Saddle points are rare compared to local minima in deep learning.
items.reality: In high dimensions, critical points with mixed curvature are common; empirical and theoretical work suggests saddles and flat regions dominate parts of the landscape gradient descent traverses.
items.example: At a random initialization for a small MLP, the Hessian often has both positive and negative eigenvalues—signature of nearby saddle structure rather than a clean minimum basin.
